rulings.Case Title: IN RE: THEODORE F SCHROEDER ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Hearing On Non-Parties' Amended Petition To Quash
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Friday, December 12, 2014, Line 1: Non-Parties PINTEREST, INC., BEN SILBERMANN, MICHELE DIGIACOMO, EVAN SHARP, PAUL SCIARRA, and JUSTIN EDMUND'S Amended Petition To Quash. Pro Tem Judge Peter Catalanotti, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: hearing required. Each party is required to bring a proposed order to the hearing. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: NAOKI NITTA, INDIVIDUALLY , AS SUCCESSOR IN VS. ELDORADO NATIONAL (KANSAS), INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Regents Of The University Of California And Wallace Richardson Motion For Summary Judgment (Duplicate Entry; For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, December 12, 2014, Line 4, DEFENDANTS REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA and WALLACE RICHARDSON'S Motion For Summary Judgment (Duplicate Entry; For Tentative Ruling Only) (Part 2 of 2) Additionally, Plaintiffs present evidence that the shuttle buses could be used on an employee's day off or to go to a location completely unrelated to a UCSF job site, such as a San Francisco Giants' game. (Plaintiffs' Ex. 3, Villalobos Depo., pp. 62:18-63:1; 124:20-23; 125:17-126:12.) As such, there is a triable issue of fact as to whether the purpose of the shuttle system was to move employees to or from their place of employment. Because UC Regents did not meet their burden on the first argument, and because Plaintiffs' opposition raises triable issues of material facts as to the second argument, the Court need not reach the issue of whether co-defendants may file an opposition to another defendant's motion for summary judgment. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. (End of Tentative Ruling) =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: NAOKI NITTA, INDIVIDUALLY , AS SUCCESSOR IN VS. ELDORADO NATIONAL (KANSAS), INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, December 12, 2014, Line 4, DEFENDANTS REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA and WALLACE RICHARDSON'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. (Part 1 of 2) Denied. Defendants The Regents of the University of California and Wallace Richardson (collectively, "UC Regents") have not met their initial burden of showing there is a complete defense to Plaintiffs' causes of action. (CCP sec. 437c(p)(2).) UC Regents argue that worker's compensation is Plaintiffs' exclusive remedy because the coming and going exception does not apply. UC Regents present no evidence that on the date of the accident Dr. Mack had any work obligations to perform at the Parnassus location. Further, UC Regents present no evidence that Dr. Mack was riding the shuttle to San Francisco General Hospital in order to go perform obligations for his job as a UCSF employee. Thus, UC Regents have not met their initial burden of establishing that Dr. Mack had arrived at work prior to boarding the shuttle bus, and was just moving from one work location to another. Alternatively, UC Regents argue that if the coming and going exception does apply, then the employer furnished transportation exception applies. UC Regents have met their initial burden by submitting evidence establishing that UCSF supplied transportation to and from its offices as a regular practice and Dr. Mack regularly took advantage of that transportation. (UMFs 1, 15; See California Cas. Indem. Exchange v. Industrial Acc. Commission (1942) 21 Cal.2d 461, 464 holding that an exception to the coming and going rule exists if "the employer¿supplied transportation to and from its office as a regular practice, and the employee regularly used that transportation.") Plaintiffs' evidence raises triable issues of material fact. "The essential prerequisite to compensation is that the danger from which the injury results be one to which he is exposed as an employee in his particular employment. This requirement is met when, as an employee and solely by reason of his relationship as such to his employer, he enters a vehicle regularly provided by his employer for the purpose of transporting him to or from the place of employment." (California Cas. Indem. Exchange, 21 Cal.2d at 466.) Plaintiffs' evidence raises triable issues as to whether Dr. Mack used the shuttle system "solely by reason of his relationship" as a UCSF employee. The evidence submitted shows that the shuttle system could be used by anyone, whether affiliated with UCSF or not. (Plaintiffs' Ex. 1, Davis Depo., pp. 139:23-140:2.) (Continued to Part 2) =(302/EHG)
